ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents an incremental but sensible advance: hierarchical (relation-level + word-level) matching with deep BiLSTMs and residual shortcuts applies known neural components in a KBQA relation-detection setting rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm. The pipeline choices (entity re-ranking, replacing the mention with a token, and treating per-entity core-chain detection without joint inference) are useful engineering contributions and differ from some prior KBQA systems, but they are not conceptually groundbreaking compared to existing relation-detection and attention/sequence-matching literature. Overall the work provides a non-trivial application and combination of existing techniques with clear empirical gains, so I rate its novelty as moderate (incremental). | B(3): The paper proposes a targeted architectural improvement (hierarchical relation- and word-level representations with deep BiLSTMs and residual connections) and a pragmatic pipeline variant (entity re-ranking + per-entity core-chain scoring) that together yield stronger empirical results. These ideas are a meaningful, non-trivial extension of existing neural relation-detection and KBQA pipelines (e.g., attention/BiCNN/STAGG-style baselines) but do not introduce a fundamentally new problem formulation or paradigm; rather they adapt and combine known components in a way that improves performance. The clear differentiation (single-entity reformatting with <e>, avoidance of joint inference) and reported gains would be of practical interest to experts, but the conceptual novelty is moderate rather than groundbreaking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents an incremental but sensible advance: hierarchical (relation-level + word-level) matching with deep BiLSTMs and residual shortcuts applies known neural components in a KBQA relation-detection setting rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm. The pipeline choices (entity re-ranking, replacing the mention with a token, and treating per-entity core-chain detection without joint inference) are useful engineering contributions and differ from some prior KBQA systems, but they are not conceptually groundbreaking compared to existing relation-detection and attention/sequence-matching literature. Overall the work provides a non-trivial application and combination of existing techniques with clear empirical gains, so I rate its novelty as moderate (incremental).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a targeted architectural improvement (hierarchical relation- and word-level representations with deep BiLSTMs and residual connections) and a pragmatic pipeline variant (entity re-ranking + per-entity core-chain scoring) that together yield stronger empirical results. These ideas are a meaningful, non-trivial extension of existing neural relation-detection and KBQA pipelines (e.g., attention/BiCNN/STAGG-style baselines) but do not introduce a fundamentally new problem formulation or paradigm; rather they adapt and combine known components in a way that improves performance. The clear differentiation (single-entity reformatting with <e>, avoidance of joint inference) and reported gains would be of practical interest to experts, but the conceptual novelty is moderate rather than groundbreaking.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments demonstrate strong empirical gains on SQ and WebQSP and compare against relevant baselines, with hyperparameters reported and a plausible end-to-end KBQA integration. However, the summary does not indicate thorough ablation studies isolating each architectural contribution (hierarchical vs. residual vs. depth) or multiple-run variance/significance testing, and code/seeds/splits/compute details appear missing which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough and a limitation (no attention-based hierarchical matching) is acknowledged, but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than exhaustive. | B(3): The experiments demonstrate strong empirical gains on two standard KBQA benchmarks (SimpleQuestions and WebQSP) and compare against multiple baselines, but evaluation is limited to only those datasets and a small set of baselines, leaving questions about generality and scalability to larger or more diverse KBQA settings. Ablation-style insight is partial: the paper analyzes the hierarchical relation/word representations and residual BiLSTM design, yet explicitly omits exploring attention-based hierarchical matching and provides little granular error analysis of failure modes for multi-relation queries. Related work appears well covered and hyperparameters are reported, but code is not mentioned and reproducibility would be aided by more implementation details and richer negative-result discussion, so the empirical substantiation is adequate but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments demonstrate strong empirical gains on SQ and WebQSP and compare against relevant baselines, with hyperparameters reported and a plausible end-to-end KBQA integration. However, the summary does not indicate thorough ablation studies isolating each architectural contribution (hierarchical vs. residual vs. depth) or multiple-run variance/significance testing, and code/seeds/splits/compute details appear missing which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough and a limitation (no attention-based hierarchical matching) is acknowledged, but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments demonstrate strong empirical gains on two standard KBQA benchmarks (SimpleQuestions and WebQSP) and compare against multiple baselines, but evaluation is limited to only those datasets and a small set of baselines, leaving questions about generality and scalability to larger or more diverse KBQA settings. Ablation-style insight is partial: the paper analyzes the hierarchical relation/word representations and residual BiLSTM design, yet explicitly omits exploring attention-based hierarchical matching and provides little granular error analysis of failure modes for multi-relation queries. Related work appears well covered and hyperparameters are reported, but code is not mentioned and reproducibility would be aided by more implementation details and richer negative-result discussion, so the empirical substantiation is adequate but not fully comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper targets an important subproblem in KBQA—relation detection—and reports state-of-the-art accuracy on both SimpleQuestions and WebQSP by introducing hierarchical relation representations and residual connections in deep BiLSTMs. From my perspective as a methodical evaluator, this is a solid, empirically validated engineering advance that is likely to be adopted by researchers working on KBQA and relation matching. However, the contribution is primarily an architectural improvement within an existing pipeline (no new paradigm or broader theoretical insight), so its influence beyond KBQA and relation-detection benchmarks is likely moderate rather than transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles a central practical bottleneck in KBQA—robust relation detection—and demonstrates measurable gains on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) benchmarks, which matters to researchers and practitioners. Its hierarchical relation representations and residual deep-BiLSTM matching provide a concrete, reproducible technique that is likely to change how relation matching is done in pipeline KBQA systems and to be incorporated into follow-up work. It also points to clear next steps (e.g., attention-based hierarchical matching) and therefore opens useful research directions, though the contribution is primarily within the KBQA/relation-detection niche rather than broadly transformative across NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important subproblem in KBQA—relation detection—and reports state-of-the-art accuracy on both SimpleQuestions and WebQSP by introducing hierarchical relation representations and residual connections in deep BiLSTMs. From my perspective as a methodical evaluator, this is a solid, empirically validated engineering advance that is likely to be adopted by researchers working on KBQA and relation matching. However, the contribution is primarily an architectural improvement within an existing pipeline (no new paradigm or broader theoretical insight), so its influence beyond KBQA and relation-detection benchmarks is likely moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a central practical bottleneck in KBQA—robust relation detection—and demonstrates measurable gains on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) benchmarks, which matters to researchers and practitioners. Its hierarchical relation representations and residual deep-BiLSTM matching provide a concrete, reproducible technique that is likely to change how relation matching is done in pipeline KBQA systems and to be incorporated into follow-up work. It also points to clear next steps (e.g., attention-based hierarchical matching) and therefore opens useful research directions, though the contribution is primarily within the KBQA/relation-detection niche rather than broadly transformative across NLP.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to several relevant recent baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, an attention variant) on the two standard KBQA benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same metric (accuracy), and reports hyperparameters—supporting largely fair comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate comprehensive ablation or variance studies isolating the contributions of hierarchical representations, depth, and residual connections nor clear discussion when prior methods are comparable; code and exact preprocessing/entity-linking details are not mentioned, which hampers full reproducibility and fairness checks. The authors do situate their approach relative to prior work and note limitations (e.g., not exploring attention-based hierarchical matching), so comparisons are good but missing some crucial analyses and reproducibility details. | B(4): The paper compares to several appropriate and recent baselines for KBQA relation detection (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention models) on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same accuracy metric, which supports fair evaluation. The authors also analyze architectural differences (hierarchical relation/word representations, residual BiLSTMs) and acknowledge limitations and future directions (e.g., attention-based hierarchical matching). Minor omissions remain — the summary does not indicate comparisons to a few alternative paradigms (strong semantic-parsing or recent end-to-end retrieval approaches) or explicit discussion when baselines perform similarly on particular splits — but overall the comparisons are solid and well-motivated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to several relevant recent baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, an attention variant) on the two standard KBQA benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same metric (accuracy), and reports hyperparameters—supporting largely fair comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate comprehensive ablation or variance studies isolating the contributions of hierarchical representations, depth, and residual connections nor clear discussion when prior methods are comparable; code and exact preprocessing/entity-linking details are not mentioned, which hampers full reproducibility and fairness checks. The authors do situate their approach relative to prior work and note limitations (e.g., not exploring attention-based hierarchical matching), so comparisons are good but missing some crucial analyses and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to several appropriate and recent baselines for KBQA relation detection (STAGG, AMPCNN, BiCNN, attention models) on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP) using the same accuracy metric, which supports fair evaluation. The authors also analyze architectural differences (hierarchical relation/word representations, residual BiLSTMs) and acknowledge limitations and future directions (e.g., attention-based hierarchical matching). Minor omissions remain — the summary does not indicate comparisons to a few alternative paradigms (strong semantic-parsing or recent end-to-end retrieval approaches) or explicit discussion when baselines perform similarly on particular splits — but overall the comparisons are solid and well-motivated.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical relation representations and residual learning improves relation detection, and that integrating it into a two-step KBQA pipeline yields state-of-the-art on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP) and supports them with experiments against reasonable baselines. Mathematical content is limited to model descriptions and appears consistent, and hyperparameters are reported. However, reproducibility and statistical rigor are insufficiently addressed (no mention of code release, random seeds, multiple runs or variance/significance testing), and the paper omits exploration of attention-based hierarchical matching which could be a relevant control; these are minor but important gaps that prevent a perfect score. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and are supported by empirical evidence: the HR-BiLSTM relation detector is evaluated against reasonable baselines on both SimpleQuestions and WebQSP and yields state-of-the-art accuracy, with reported hyperparameters. There are no formal mathematical proofs required for the architecture, and the methodology appears sound (appropriate baselines and ablations), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or released code for reproducibility. Overall the experiments substantiate the conclusions, with only minor concerns about reported significance and reproducibility that do not undermine the technical validity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical relation representations and residual learning improves relation detection, and that integrating it into a two-step KBQA pipeline yields state-of-the-art on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP) and supports them with experiments against reasonable baselines. Mathematical content is limited to model descriptions and appears consistent, and hyperparameters are reported. However, reproducibility and statistical rigor are insufficiently addressed (no mention of code release, random seeds, multiple runs or variance/significance testing), and the paper omits exploration of attention-based hierarchical matching which could be a relevant control; these are minor but important gaps that prevent a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and are supported by empirical evidence: the HR-BiLSTM relation detector is evaluated against reasonable baselines on both SimpleQuestions and WebQSP and yields state-of-the-art accuracy, with reported hyperparameters. There are no formal mathematical proofs required for the architecture, and the methodology appears sound (appropriate baselines and ablations), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or released code for reproducibility. Overall the experiments substantiate the conclusions, with only minor concerns about reported significance and reproducibility that do not undermine the technical validity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP task (KBQA relation detection) using well-known datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP), standard baselines, and expected evaluation metrics, so the topic and audience align well with a top NLP/ML venue. The methodology (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical relation representations and residual connections) and integration into a KBQA pipeline are appropriate for the community and evaluated against competitive baselines. Minor concerns for top-tier fit are absence of stated code release and limited exploration of attention-based alternatives (noted by authors), which slightly weakens reproducibility and breadth of empirical evaluation but do not preclude venue relevance. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles a central KBQA problem (relation detection) with a novel HR-BiLSTM hierarchical matching architecture and residual learning, and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP). The methodological choices, evaluation metrics, and baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, etc.) are exactly those valued by the community, and the work is clearly aimed at researchers building robust KBQA and relation-detection systems. From a KBQA research perspective the integration into a practical two-step pipeline and focus on multi-relation queries make the paper directly relevant to the venue’s audience.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP task (KBQA relation detection) using well-known datasets (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP), standard baselines, and expected evaluation metrics, so the topic and audience align well with a top NLP/ML venue. The methodology (HR-BiLSTM with hierarchical relation representations and residual connections) and integration into a KBQA pipeline are appropriate for the community and evaluated against competitive baselines. Minor concerns for top-tier fit are absence of stated code release and limited exploration of attention-based alternatives (noted by authors), which slightly weakens reproducibility and breadth of empirical evaluation but do not preclude venue relevance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles a central KBQA problem (relation detection) with a novel HR-BiLSTM hierarchical matching architecture and residual learning, and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (SimpleQuestions, WebQSP). The methodological choices, evaluation metrics, and baselines (STAGG, AMPCNN, etc.) are exactly those valued by the community, and the work is clearly aimed at researchers building robust KBQA and relation-detection systems. From a KBQA research perspective the integration into a practical two-step pipeline and focus on multi-relation queries make the paper directly relevant to the venue’s audience.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to the HR-BiLSTM method and experiments on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP; main contributions (hierarchical matching, residual learning, and KBQA integration) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, which helps reproducibility-minded readers, and the related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is generally good, though the authors note an unexplored direction (attention-based hierarchical matching) suggesting a minor opportunity to clarify limitations and future work more concretely. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized with a clear progression from introduction through method (HR-BiLSTM) to experiments on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP and a concise conclusion. The main contributions are stated early, notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though the paper could more explicitly discuss the choice not to include attention-based hierarchical matching (noted as future work) which slightly reduces clarity around methodological decisions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to the HR-BiLSTM method and experiments on SimpleQuestions and WebQSP; main contributions (hierarchical matching, residual learning, and KBQA integration) are stated early. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is generally good, though the authors note an unexplored direction (attention-based hierarchical matching) suggesting a minor opportunity to clarify limitations and future work more concretely.

The main contributions are stated early, notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though the paper could more explicitly discuss the choice not to include attention-based hierarchical matching (noted as future work) which slightly reduces clarity around methodological decisions.

main_claims: An improved relation detection model (HR-BiLSTM) using hierarchical matching between questions and relations with residual learning.
Using relation-level and word-level relation representations and deep bidirectional LSTMs to better match question and relation information.

Integration of the improved relation detector into a simple two-step KBQA system (entity re-ranking and per-entity relation detection) yields state-of-the-art results on both single-relation (SimpleQuestions) and multi-relation (WebQSP) benchmarks.
_summary.methodology: They propose a hierarchical recurrent neural network (HR-BiLSTM) that builds relation-level and word-level relation representations and uses deep bidirectional LSTMs with residual (shortcut) connections to match questions and relation name sequences; the detector is integrated into a KBQA pipeline via entity re-ranking and per-entity relation (core-chain) scoring.
baselines: Attention model (Parikh et al., 2016) one-way variation
limitations: They did not explore attention-based hierarchical matching and note that hierarchical matching with attention may work better for long sequences (left for future work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Knowledge Base Question Answering / Relation Detection

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Evaluator
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating neural NLP systems, including production QA pipelines and open-source reproducible benchmarks.
traits: looks for comprehensive ablation studies that isolate contribution of each model component (e.g., hierarchical representations, residual connections, depth)
values reproducibility: requests exact hyperparameters, training regimen, dataset splits, random seeds, and code/compute requirements
carefully inspects baselines and experimental controls (ensures comparisons use same data preprocessing, embeddings, and entity linking)
evaluates statistical rigor: expects multiple runs, variance reporting, and significance testing for claimed improvements
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental soundness and implementation transparency, judging whether the empirical claims are convincingly supported and whether the work can be reproduced.

reviewer_b.name: KBQA Landscape Analyst
reviewer_b.background: Research scientist specializing in knowledge-base question answering and semantic parsing, with a track record of papers on relation detection, multi-hop QA, and KB integration.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior KBQA and relation-detection methods and how the approach advances the field
examines practical impact: scalability to large KBs, handling of multi-relation queries, and real-world applicability beyond benchmark tuning
looks for qualitative analysis: error types, cases where hierarchical matching helps or fails, and insights into linguistic phenomena captured by the model
values clear articulation of limitations and realistic comparisons to alternative paradigms (e.g., semantic parsers, end-to-end retrieval models)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's significance and novelty within KBQA, emphasizing how the proposed ideas change practice or understanding in the area and what follow-up work they enable.
